coredata.dc:title: Antifungal efficacy of Au@ carbon dots nanoconjugates against opportunistic fungal pathogen, Candida albicans
coredata.dc:creator: Priyadarshini, Eepsita
Rawat, Kamla
Prasad, Tulika
Bohidar, H.B.
coredata.dc:description: In the current study, we have investigated the toxicological effect of a novel hydrophilic nanoconjugate gold@carbon dot (Au@CD) and carbon dots (CDs) on the opportunistic fungal pathogen, Candida albicans. A homogenous experimental analysis was conducted for determining the toxicity of Au@CDs nanoconjugates of five different sizes ranging from 22 ± 2 to 35 ± 3 nm prepared using the carbon dots of mean hydrodynamic radius 12 ± 1 nm. The smallest size of nanoconjugate was synthesized using 0.3 mg ml−1 HAuCl4 precursor. Our study for the first time, conclusively establishes the size-dependent toxicity effect of these characterized nanoconjugates against the abovementioned fungal pathogen. The MIC80 value of smaller sized Au@CDs nanoconjugates, S1–S3 samples were 250, 500 and 500 μg ml−1, respectively, while nanoconjugates of Rh diameter greater than 30 nm (S4 and S5 samples) did not show any toxicity. The results thus demonstrate that alteration in composition (carbon vs Au@CDs) exhibits a profound effect on the susceptibility of Candida albicans cells. While a size-dependent toxicity was observed for the nanoconjugates, CDs were found to be quite toxic owing to their small size which facilitated their entry into the cells and challenged the biocompatibility of carbon allotropes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold-carbon dot nanoconjugates
Candida albicans
Fluorescence
Cytotoxicity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots (CD) and of Au nanoparticles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of (a) UV–vis absorption and (b) fluorescence spectra of Au@CDs nanoconjugates synthesized using different concentrations of HAuCl4 precursor. Note the increase in absorbance and fluorescence intensity quenching with increasing concentration of the precursor.

(a) TEM image of Au@CDs (S1 sample). The average size of the particles was 10 ± 0.13 nm as shown in the particle size histogram (b). Elemental mapping of the nanoconjugates confirmed the presence of Au and C peaks (d) HRTEM image revealed the d-spacing of 0.22 nm between the atomic layers of nanoconjugates.

(a) Graph showing the comparative inhibition percentage of the synthesized Au@CDs nanoconjugates of different sizes. (b) The MIC80 value obtained for the particles. Note that CD, GNP and CA represent carbon dots, gold nanoparticles and citric acid, respectively.
(a) Growth curve of Candida albicans in presence of CDs and Au@CDs of five different sizes (b) Graph showing the relative percentage of cell death in treated cells after 24 h of treatment.
(a) Growth curve of Candida albicans after treatment with different concentrations of CDs (b) Graph showing the correlation between relative percentage of cell death and concentration of CDs.
Physical characteristics of Au@CDs nanoconjugates synthesized at different HAuCl4 concentration.

head.ce:title: Antifungal efficacy of Au@ carbon dots nanoconjugates against opportunistic fungal pathogen, Candida albicans

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Sciences, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Special Centre for Nano Sciences, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Inter University Accelerator Centre, New Delhi, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Instrument Research Facility, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Special Centre for Nano Sciences, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the current study, we have investigated the toxicological effect of a novel hydrophilic nanoconjugate gold@carbon dot (Au@CD) and carbon dots (CDs) on the opportunistic fungal pathogen, Candida albicans. A homogenous experimental analysis was conducted for determining the toxicity of Au@CDs nanoconjugates of five different sizes ranging from 22 ± 2 to 35 ± 3 nm prepared using the carbon dots of mean hydrodynamic radius 12 ± 1 nm. The smallest size of nanoconjugate was synthesized using 0.3 mg ml−1 HAuCl4 precursor. Our study for the first time, conclusively establishes the size-dependent toxicity effect of these characterized nanoconjugates against the abovementioned fungal pathogen. The MIC80 value of smaller sized Au@CDs nanoconjugates, S1–S3 samples were 250, 500 and 500 μg ml−1, respectively, while nanoconjugates of Rh diameter greater than 30 nm (S4 and S5 samples) did not show any toxicity. The results thus demonstrate that alteration in composition (carbon vs Au@CDs) exhibits a profound effect on the susceptibility of Candida albicans cells. While a size-dependent toxicity was observed for the nanoconjugates, CDs were found to be quite toxic owing to their small size which facilitated their entry into the cells and challenged the biocompatibility of carbon allotropes.

ce:section.ce:para: Fungal infections pose a challenging threat to human health and affect millions of individuals [1,2]. More than 17 species of Candida are responsible for causing both superficial and life threatening systemic mycoses and out of these, Candida albicans is a major fungal pathogen accounting for more than 90% of invasive fungal infections and most common cause of nosocomial infections. Candida albicans is normally a dimorphic commensal organism residing as part of the natural flora of healthy people. However, it undergoes a transition from commensal to pathogenic form in immunocompromised individuals [3]. Growth of Candida biofilms on medical devices has emerged as a major challenge for hospital settings leading to secondary fungal infections and resulting in morbidity and mortality of immuno-compromised patients [4]. Increase in prevalence of Candidiasis, emergence of drug resistance and lower availability of antifungal drugs result in failure of treatment and recalcitrant infections [5]. Therefore, there is a constant demand for the development of new generation antifungal agents with superior antifungal properties and minimal host toxicity [6].

Despite availability of a limited number of antifungals and increase in incidence of multidrug resistance (MDR), inadequate attention has been given to the development of broad spectrum antifungals. Nanoscience brings new tools and capabilities to every aspect of these challenges. However, in the last few decades, nanotechnology has brought new tools and capabilities to address every aspect of these challenges and nanomedicine has made a rapid and wide impact on healthcare. Numerous investigations have demonstrated the promising potential of various nanomaterials to combat microbial drug resistance and improve the microbial therapeutics. Nanoparticles are a potent class of materials for developing efficacious antifungal agents. Out of all other metal nanoparticles, silver nanoparticles have been widely investigated and emerged as an excellent antifungal and antibacterial agent [7,8]. U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has approved silver for burn for burn wound dressings and is toxic for the human body only at higher concentrations. At lower concentration, silver does not exhibit deleterious effect but at high concentration, it is shown to inhibit protein and DNA synthesis [9]. Furthermore, persistent ingestion of silver causes argyrosis and argria [10,11]. However, safer clinical use of silver still requires more clinical investigations and research. Although, novel nanostructures provide unique tools for addressing both bacterial and fungal infections, most of the nanomaterials have been the focus of study for drug delivery especially for treatment of cancer. Lots of scope still remains for designing nanomaterials that can be administered to the bloodstream or in the presence of serum or plasma while maintaining an ability to interact or engage microbes directly. In this study, we have evaluated the potential antifungal property of Au@CD nanoconjugates against common fungal pathogen, Candida albicans. These nanoconjugates represent a novel class of nanomaterials with the combined properties of gold nanoparticles and carbon dots [12]. These conjugates also possess fascinating optical as well as fluorescent properties and are a new kind of nanomaterial whose applicability appears highly promising. Carbon dots have aroused significant interest because of their remarkable aqueous solubility, biocompatibility, size and wavelength dependent photoluminescence properties [13–15]. Similarly, the size and shape dependent Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) optical property of gold nanoparticles has been successfully used in catalysis, sensing, detection and biomedicines [16]. Recently, some reports have suggested the advantages of metal/carbon-nanohybrids over metal nanoparticles because of the exceptional capability of carbon dots to reduce metal salts and in addition also to serve as stabilizing agents for the metal nanoparticle after their synthesis [17]. The synthesis of Au@CDs nanoconjugates, with Au core and CDs shell will incorporate the dual properties resulting in nanoconjugates with scope and suitability for broad use in surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) studies, bioimaging, labeling and antimicrobial agents on one platform.

The objective of this work was to evaluate the effect of Au@CDs nanoconjugates as potential antifungal agents against Candida albicans and to investigate its size-dependent toxicity against fungal cells. Candida was chosen as the representative model for fungal pathogens and the results obtained is most likely to have clinical relevance for other fungal pathogens. We also compared the relative growth of cells of Candida albicans in the presence of C-dots and Au@CDs nanoconjugates. The antifungal potential of nanoconjugates of five different sizes was assessed and a comparative analysis was performed. These nanoconjugates differed in absorption and fluorescence yield, depending on the concentration of HAuCl4 precursor used for synthesis. The present study contributes towards the generation of novel antifungals and is a major step towards the development of new antifungal coatings which can be further investigated for their biofilm inhibition potential.

Tetrachloroauric acid (HAuCl4) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA. All other chemicals used in the study were purchased from SRL, India. All the chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. Standard stock solutions of HAuCl4 was prepared with deionised water and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots (CD) and of Au nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized via microwave assisted method using citric acid and PEG as precursors. Briefly, 1.1 g of citric acid and 1 g PEG was dissolved in 15 ml deionized water and stirred for 30 min. After stirring, the solution was heated in a domestic microwave oven at 800 W for 6 min. During the process, the color of the solution changed from colorless to dark brown indicating the formation of CDs. The aliquot was dispersed in deionized water and stored at 4 °C for future use.

Au nanoparticles were synthesized using trisodium citrate as the reducing and stabilizing agent. For synthesis, 0.5% (w/v) trisodium citrate was heated to boiling and aqueous solution of HAuCl4 was added to it drop-wise such that its final concentration in the solution was 0.06 mg ml−1. The reactant solution was heated till a stable wine red suspension of Au nanoparticles were obtained.

The synthesis of Au@CDs was performed as per the method described in our earlier study [12]. Briefly, 5 ml aqueous solution of prepared CDs (5.3 mg ml−1) was heated to 100 °C with continuous stirring and aqueous solution of HAuCl4 was added to it. The concentration of HAuCl4 solution was varied so as to synthesize Au@CDs of different sizes. In brief, 100, 75, 50, 25 and 12.5 μl of stock HAuCl4 solution was added to the prepared CD dispersions, such that the concentration of HAuCl4 in the reacting solution was 0.03, 0.06, 0.12, 0.18 and 0.24 mg ml−1 respectively. These samples were labeled as S1, S2, S3, S4 and S5 respectively. The reacting solution was kept at 100 °C to yield a stable pink/purple colored suspension of Au@CDs depending on the nanoconjugate size.

UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Cary 60 spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA) in the spectral range of 200 – 900 nm. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA). Quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized nanoconjugates was determined using DAPI dilactate as the reference sample (QY of 43% in water). QY of the samples were calculated using the following formula. QY = (FArQYr)/(FrA), where F and A represents the integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbance of the sample respectively and Fr and Ar represent the corresponding reference values [18]. The size, morphology and 3D elemental mapping of particles were performed using high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, USA) and scanning electron microscope using a JEOL 2100F microscope equipped with a tungsten filament (200 kV, 150,000X), Japan.

The hydrodynamic size Rh of these nanoconjugates was determined using a dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument, and Rh was calculated from the Stokes-Einstein equation [18]. Zeta potential analysis was performed on a ZEECOM instrument (Microtech, Japan) which measured the electrophoretic mobility directly, see Rawat and Bohidar [19] for more details. All the procedures were performed at room temperature (20 °C) unless otherwise stated.

For determining antifungal potential of the Au@CD nanoconjugate, wild type strain of Candida albicans strain was used [20]. The strain was maintained on liquid broth (YEPD medium) constituting of 2% (w/v) Dextrose, 1% (w/v) Yeast extract and 2% (w/v) Peptone and 2.5% (w/v) agar for solid media. The fungal strains were preserved at −80 °C in 15% (v/v) glycerol stock solution. Cells were revived on YEPD agar plates and stored at 4 °C. For experimental analysis, cells were cultured for 14–16 h by incubating streaked YEPD agar plates at 30 °C.

Broth micro-dilution assay method was used to determine the Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC) of the Candida cells towards the Au@CDs nanoconjugates in accordance to reported procedure [21,22]. MIC80 is defined as the lowest concentration of drug which is capable of inhibiting almost 80% of cell growth in comparison to control set (without any drug treatment). Briefly exponential growing log phase cells (grown for 14–16 h on agar plates) were re-suspended in 0.9% NaCl solution so as to give an optical density of 0.1 at 600 nm. This corresponds to around 0.5–1 × 106 cells ml−1 in the medium. The re-suspended cells were then diluted 100 times in fresh YEPD medium. 100 μl of the diluted cell suspension was added to the wells of 96-well plate containing an equal volume of medium (100 μl in each well) and the respective Au@CDs nanoconjugates in different dilutions. Control set with the YEPD media only and cells without nanoconjugates was also maintained simultaneously. The cells were then allowed to grow by incubating the plates at 30 °C for 48 h. After the endpoint the reading was recorded at OD620 in a microplate reader. MIC80 and percentage of inhibition was then determined. Separate sets of experiments were conducted for all sets of Au@CD nanoconjugates synthesized. Citric acid, gold nanoparticles (GNPs) and CDs were also used for comparative analysis.

Furthermore, spot assay was also performed using CDs as the precursor to validate their antifungal property. Standard spot assay was used as a confirmation of antimicrobial activity by determining the spot size and density distribution as a function of dilution [21,23]. The spot assay performed using CDs was compared with control (grown in absence of CDs) to ascertain the toxicity of CDs. For this CDs were mixed with YEPD agar media and poured into Petri plates. Upon solidification, 5 fold serially diluted Candida albicans culture (optical density of 0.1 at 600 nm) was spotted on YEPD plates.

To analyze the effect of CDs and Au@CDs on the growth of Candida albicans, we performed the growth curve analysis. For this, fresh Candida albicans cultured for 16 h in YEPD agar plate was re-suspended in 0.9% NaCl such that the optical density was 0.1 at 600 nm. The cells were then re-suspended in 25 ml of fresh YEPD broth medium and treated with CDs and Au@CDs nanoconjugates of 5 different sizes respectively. A control set without any nanoconjugate was also taken and the cultures were incubated in a shaker incubator maintained at 30 °C. Aliquots of sample were drawn at regular time intervals and optical density was measured at 600 nm to determine the growth curve. In yet another study, the cells were treated with five different concentrations (25, 50, 100, 150 and 200 μg ml−1) of CDs to investigate a dose-dependent effect of CDs on the growth of Candida albicans cells.

To further validate the antifungal effect of the nanoconjugates, we analyzed the interaction of Au@CDs with Candida albicans. For the assay, we treated Au@CDs colloidal suspension with gradually increasing volume of cell suspension (optical density maintained at 0.1) and recorded the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra before and after treatment.

Au@CDs nanoconjugates were synthesized via a two-step procedure involving the carbonization of citric acid and PEG in a microwave assisted method, followed by thermal treatment of generated CDs and HAuCl4 precursors. Formation of these nanoconjugates was confirmed from the change in color of solution to pink/purple depending on particle size. Variations in the concentration of HAuCl4 precursor resulted in synthesis of particles of different size, reflected from the appearance of surface plasmon resonance peak at different locations. Fluorescence spectrophotometric analysis of the synthesized CDs showed an intense fluorescent peak at 454 nm upon excitation at 334 nm Fig. S1 (Supplementary Information). S0 denotes the CD samples only. On analyzing the fluorescence spectra of the nanoconjugates, quenching of fluorescence intensity was observed with increasing HAuCl4 concentration. The nanoconjugates synthesized using 0.3 mg ml−1 of HAuCl4 precursor revealed maximum fluorescence quantum yield of 30.2% and those synthesized with 0.24 mg ml−1 had a quantum yield of 8.41%. Fig. 1 (a) and (b) shows the comparative UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of these nanoconjugates.

Zeta potential analysis of the nanoconjugates showed that the particles had an overall negative surface charge in the range of −9 to −11 mV. The presence of negative charge provides electrostatic repulsion amongst the particles thereby, preventing their aggregation and providing dispersion stability. The mean hydrodynamic radius (Rh) of the nanoconjugates was determined from DLS analysis. The Rh values clearly revealed that with increasing concentration of HAuCl4, there was an increase in the mean hydrodynamic size of the particles. The high Rh value of nanoconjugates was because of the presence of solvation layer when dispersed in an aqueous medium. Table 1 shows the physical characteristics of CDs, and Au@CDs nanoconjugates synthesized at varying precursor concentration with their samples IDs.

TEM analysis was conducted to determine the size and morphology of nanoconjugates. It was found that Au@CDs nanoconjugates synthesized using 0.03 mg ml−1 of HAuCl4 were mainly quasi-spherical although some other shapes like triangular and elongated were also present (Fig. 2 a). Size distribution of the nanoconjugates was in the range of ≈2–16 nm with the average size of 10 ± 1 nm (Fig. 2b). Elemental analysis of Au@CDs nanoconjugates confirmed the presence of Au and C. The appearance of C peak was also due to the carbon-coated Cu grid used in the instrument as substrate. Therefore, line and 3D elemental mapping was performed which suggested that the CDs coat the Au core, and were present on the surface to make the nanoconjugate structure (Fig. 2c). HRTEM image revealed that the d-spacing between the atomic layers of the nanoconjugates was 0.22 nm which is shown in Fig. 2d.

It is well known that the size and morphology of nanoparticles (NPs) have a significant effect on the corresponding optical, electronic and catalytic properties of particles. Therefore, the nature of material and size of particles are major determining factors in their antimicrobial property. We therefore, explored the antifungal potential of CDs and Au@CDs of five different sizes on the viability of Candida albicans. The fungicidal activity of CDs and nanoconjugates was determined via standard micro-dilution method with a dilution of samples from 500 to 0.97 μg ml−1. The obtained results suggested that the CDs with an average Rh of 12 ± 1 nm showed an MIC80 at 62.5 μg ml−1 concentration (Fig. 3 a). In a recent study conducted by Dong et al. [24], CDs exhibited toxicity towards both Gram negative bacteria E. coli and Gram positive bacteria Bacillus subtilis at a minimum concentration of 64 μg ml−1. In yet another study, synthesized quaternized CDs showed a high selectivity to gram positive bacteria, S. aureus with a MIC of 8 μg ml−1 [25]. For comparative analysis, GNP (gold nanoparticles) and citric acid (precursor for synthesis) were used as controls sets and they did not show any toxic effect on the Candida cells. Simultaneously, we assessed the antifungal property of Au@CDs nanoconjugates and observed a size-dependent toxicity. On comparing the toxic effect of different size of Au@CDs nanoconjugates, we found that toxicity decreased with particle size as was hypothesized. The smaller size particles had a higher toxicity in comparison to the larger nanoconjugates (Fig. 3a). The MIC80 value of S1–S3 samples were 250, 500 and 500 μg ml−1 respectively, while S4 and S5 samples did not show significant toxicity (Fig. 3b).

The results suggested that CDs were more toxic in comparison to Au@CDs nanoconjugates. Literature suggest concentration dependent toxic effect of CDs on the growth of E. coli and the necessity to modulate the synthesis of CDs for controlling its toxicity and enhancing its biocompatibility [26] for future biological applications. The high toxicity of CDs is because of their small size which allows their easy penetration into the fungal cells and augment production of Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) resulting in cell damage. The smaller size nanoconjugates were also observed to be toxic, again because of smaller size and the presence of CDs on their surface. The number of CD particles on Au surface is more in the smaller sized particles compared to larger ones suggesting that the toxicity came from the CD component of nanoconjugate. We have already shown that the QY increases with decreasing concentration of HAuCl4 precursor. Additionally, it is already stated that the control GNP samples did not show any toxicity and this might be the probable reason behind the decrease in toxicity of Au@CDs nanoconjugates compared to CDs.

Additionally, spot assay provided additional confirmation on the toxic nature of CDs. Five-fold serial dilution of the fungal cells was spotted on CD-treated YEPD agar plates and observed after 48 h of incubation. Distinct growth difference was observed with complete inhibition of cell growth in the last plate suggested the high susceptibility of Candida albicans to CDs Fig. S2 (Supplementary Information).

Antifungal activity of CDs, and Au@CDs nanoconjugates was studied through evaluation of cell growth by recording the optical density of cells at regular intervals. The growth inhibition profile for CDs and nanoconjugates was recorded with respect to time, which suggested a variation in growth curve in presence of CDs and Au@CDs nanoconjugates. CDs treated cells showed almost continuous inhibition of growth while the small size nanoconjugates prolonged the lag phase of growth (Fig. 4 a). This suggests the higher sensitivity of Candida albicans to CDs which is in concurrence with the micro-dilution assay data. Additionally, 55, 44 and 40% cell death was observed respectively in cells grown with CDs and S1, S2 (Au@CDs nanoconjugates after 24 h of incubation (Fig. 4b)).

Furthermore, we conducted colony counting assay to confirm the results. For this, on completion of 24 h of incubation, 100 μl of the treated samples were plated on YEPD plates and incubated for 48 h. The colonies obtained were counted after completion of 48 h and the results demonstrated a drastic reduction in the number of colonies in cells treated with S1, S2 and CD in comparison to the untreated control cells (Fig. S3, Supplementary Information). The colony forming units (CFU ml−1) were 1990, 1560, 1400, 980, 920, 760 and 330 CFU ml−1 for control, S5, S4, S3, S2, S1 and CD, respectively.

We further analyzed, dose-dependent toxicity of CDs on the growth of Candida albicans. The data represented in Fig. 5 (a) reveals the dose-dependent effect on growth and complete cell growth inhibition was observed at higher particle concentrations above 100 μg ml−1. At relatively lower concentration, delayed lag phase was seen suggesting the requirement of a relatively longer time for the cells to acclimatize to the particles. A direct correlation was noticed between the concentration of CDs used and the relative percentage of cell death as evident in Fig. 5(b).

Comparison of the antifungal property of CDs and Au@CDs nanoconjugates clearly showed that the CDs inhibited fungal growth at a lower concentration compared to the nanoconjugates. Additionally, observed dose-dependent toxicity of CDs suggested effective fungal cell killing at higher concentration and complete inhibition of cell growth under such conditions. Furthermore, variability in the inhibition activity was noticed in the case of nanoconjugates which can be ascribed to size dependence and CDs content on the nanoconjugate surface. The smaller size particles were more toxic and prolonged the lag phase of cell growth.

It has been proposed that the microbial cells come in contact with the nanoparticles either due to the release of metal ions in the surrounding or by their entry into the microbial cells, resulting in the intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, organelle and DNA damage or disruption of cell membrane integrity [27]. However, the complete mechanism behind the antimicrobial property of nanomaterial is not completely understood and needs further investigations.

In the current study, we present a comprehensive analysis to conclude that Au@CDs can act as antifungal agent against the fungal pathogen, Candida albicans. Five different size of particles were synthesized which was confirmed from DLS and TEM data. MIC value showed that GNPs and citric acid were not toxic to the Candida cells while the synthesized C-dots showed 80% growth inhibition of cells at 62.5 μg ml−1. A clear evidence of nanoconjugate size-dependent toxicity was observed in this study. While the larger size particles were not at all toxic, small size particles with average diameter of 30, 27.5 and 21.8 showed MIC80 at 250, 500 and 500 μg ml−1 concentrations, respectively. The toxicity was presumably due to combined effect of size and presence of CDs on the particle surface. Thus, Au@CDs nanoconjugates of a preferable size can act as an effective alternative to conventionally used antifungal agents. Worthwhile, to mention our results present a completely new and novel approach towards the development of antifungal agents, which has not been reported so far. Additionally, these can be used as coatings on medical devices since they are associated with the advantages of ease of functionalization and stability.
Not applicable.
EMR/2015/001827, EMR/2016/004868, and DST-Inspire Faculty award from Department of Science and Technology, Government of India.
The authors declare no conflict of financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by a DST-PURSE-II funding. The authors thank Advanced Instrument Research Facility of the University for TEM facility. KR acknowledges receipt of DST-Inspire Faculty award from Department of Science and Technology, Government of India.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2018.01.006.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
